text: I'm sharing a robotic tap recurrent inguinal hernia repair.
This gentleman had an open repair forty-something years ago. This was a primary repair, no mesh, and about four years prior to this had a robotic prostatectomy.
Very symptomatic hernia and I chose to do a tap repair.
The surgeon begins creating a flap during the robotic TAPP recurrent inguinal hernia repair, following standard procedural steps.
As the dissection progresses towards the pubic tubercle, the surgeon encounters visibly increased scarring in the tissue.
I left this mostly unedited as I'm seeking more guidance as I'm doing more and more of these cases and getting a little bit more comfortable with the technique.
The surgeon approaches the pubic bone area, encountering significant scarring, and carefully maintains dissection close to the interior abdominal wall.
The surgeon carefully exposes the pubic bone, closely following the periosteum to avoid injury to the bladder, which is likely adhered to the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection in a scarred area, revealing an Ethabon suture, likely from a previous high ligation during a non-mesh hernia repair.
The surgeon works within Bogros's space, which appears as a virgin plane, attempting to mobilize the hernia sac. The anatomical structures are more clearly visible from this angle.
The surgeon identifies the appendix tucked within the hernia, which may be symptomatic for the patient. The vas deferens is also visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully dissects near the pubic bone, maintaining close proximity to the periosteum to avoid injury to the bladder, which is presumed to be inferior to the current working area. Hemlock clips from a previous procedure are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon has completely reduced the hernia sac and is parietalizing the cord structures. The search for a lipoma begins, as indicated by the CAT scan showing a small lipoma present.

The surgeon considers further inferior and medial dissection in the scarred tissue area but decides that sufficient space has been created for mesh placement in the indirect hernia repair. The mesh is positioned in the prepared landing zone.
The surgeon places a sixteen by twelve anatomic right ProGrip mesh over the hernia defect, ensuring it covers the area adequately.

The surgeon closes the flap and pulls out the inverted hernia sac, revealing a sliding component of the appendix. An angiocath is used to remove air or gas from the pocket, marking the end of the procedure.
